# An Interview with Ben Thompson at the MoffettNathanson Media, Internet & Communications Conference (Stratechery Interview 5-14-2026)

**From:** Ben Thompson <email@stratechery.com>  
**Date:** 2026-05-14T10:02:52.000Z

---

An interview with me about the implications of the compute shortage on Aggregation Theory, consumer AI, and more.

Good morning,

This week’s Stratechery Interview is with me! On Wednesday I returned to MoffettNathanson’s annual Media, Internet & Communications Conference in New York City. While the conference as a whole is for MoffettNathanson’s subscribers, I am pleased to make the session with me available to Stratechery subscribers as well, just as I did in 2025 ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-ben-thompson-at-the-moffettnathanson-media-internet-and-communications-conference-2/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..FfgWb6ut7yoe59x8SJ8AuCI0K__AMOswhxf8ir_p3_WUUEn8Khy3g5_yDFGuxag8hdmsVmoWZBO1Q1vxp16YoiwaEr87yA3XYGglnR2KRsi1AfmNUsTEO6GcEod4-HZ8LmRkONOjZekJXTRbx4ogeGfHXHH1SYCr9WiQszLMxXdgiVVOEaKcGK_c3HmFz4Tcgn9MS_7tq2RGFYLqb6TlIHW3RgTBwBHprMcJmWUBM-xnGBJsY48Te0W_PAWq5ZXhs-2T8B2limlN121AcmdNz8TypQutroUO_RAvgdAQp4FK034TW71QLMUXooL_8_vfVzZNIkeXNWagkL1a-3HQnw ) , 2024 ( https://stratechery.com/2024/an-interview-with-ben-thompson-at-the-moffettnathanson-media-internet-and-communications-conference/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..pQQ3qpSa1bLaNwoXcSuMiK7YqABkdqT5ayuz8t4zT159Kj1XfIpixtXIYf1p7nuxdZ9ghfFC2WLlPCywcJcKwflvzRwu3h3j8GFT31tLb93EqijNXYVwnUSdvVx_h9WaKFumabBZ9gMVKOkeAfT79o2yGDty-ZykXPt3H2LHjxbGEZHj6mHaJj-v6M1F6rZOUjtnoEDwoovikeoBZRHbAXTVxnQUWYwA0fyjvkuWbbtb-tg8_OO4l2dJd4jlf0pF6Ww2pKkCZjCm40ljvWZiN_x0CYFH27LunI41nbZ-0UYLOXNmLYGJu2LVO7Cv3HCIZiQegNxjXwNHFhUo3QB0Iw ) , and 2023 ( https://stratechery.com/2023/an-interview-with-ben-thompson-at-the-moffettnathanson-technology-media-and-telecom-conference/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..H4jKeI52t9JWaGpxhJ2ynkNnHrZ3U7L_JBIr6p9F6LGZUaxdWMcLkLXVvfY8KX4Hh8C7Gbpmrts3NqV8As_O3ALbvasU_hFn8ZgstoqDvlig8YWYCNIg_Ba87AWXcpY3J-v6hDzRgjT7ld67FErwOZMS6BsCesCLakdECfCe5BpbdrhQRQddiwz4Et6rMbPcpGdrysee2ygsDFtKrZjwrbBOHg4F0ymCBKtR6SbakdRsrx_phnz2tjrkRmNh7OdqPXEDF-7U470ZirYqmHyKocvJ8bAvuxWKhZIbGF9ghYBl2yQ3q-QGSqq8XM031qmyMGVVlI5Vl8a-2buukoL5zA ).

This year we of course discussed AI, but from a variety of different perspectives. First, what are the implications of there being a compute shortage on traditional Stratechery premises like zero marginal costs, and what do I mean when I distinguish between answer and agentic inference? Then, we attack consumer AI from a number of perspectives: how would things look different if OpenAI embraced advertising sooner, and why is it possible to still be bullish about Meta? Plus, why I’m still bullish about humans and shopping. After that we discuss Apple and a number of rapid-fire questions, including enterprise software, semiconductors, and how AI has changed how I work.

As a reminder, all Stratechery content, including interviews, is available as a podcast; click the link at the top of this email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.

On to the Interview:

An Interview with Ben Thompson at the MoffettNathanson Technology, Media, and Telecom Conference
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This interview is lightly edited for clarity.

*Topics:*

The Compute Shortage ( #compute-shortage ) | Consumer AI ( #consumer-ai ) | Apple ( #apple ) | Rapid Fire ( #rapid-fire )

The Compute Shortage

*Michael Nathanson: Our next guest needs no introduction. This is, I think, your fifth time being here [Editor’s Note: Fourth]. This year’s flight was shorter, he came from Wisconsin, where he’s moved to, and we are honored to have Ben Thompson with us. And, you know, Craig [Moffett] doesn’t share praise easily, and he has said to us many times that there’s no one who’s a better thinker or better writer and the amount of volume you’re able to generate every week is mind-boggling because we have to do the same thing, and you’ve lapped all of us. Ben, thank you for being here, we’re honored for you to be here. Our goal this year is to get you guys to ask questions, which means we’ll speak less up here. There’ll be a QR code on the screens, and I’ll read the questions when we get through our questions. Ben, thank you so much, I wish you could be here longer.*

Thank you, happy to be here.

*Craig Moffett: I want to kick it off, and I started to write this question, and then as I wrote the question, I had a little epiphany, “Oh, there’s an interesting place to take this”, and I started by thinking, “Let’s get you to riff on whether AI is a winner-take-all business”, but behind that question, there is so much of what you’ve written about and what has always motivated me personally about the businesses we cover, is that they are zero marginal cost businesses and it creates that winner-take-all dynamic ( https://stratechery.com/2015/aggregation-theory/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..ejqokh_ofyMwMez47787ZqEX7WmlM8tzpcjMtYsxjrdRaYiKaomZKvkzhYqTwYhNq-yAinWPIcfMUvzx1v7LO-Br3wCpysMyuw-LWzKP7kUTFuypEr66Szourqyh-FwBnI91xIxNIH-zm104YNAunllsLj6v1jaWMwAu7Lnbbqmkl55OPd3k83HXEUzLlNM30NJPCXG3Vj5repaYoc28ciSntvZ6g6wis39BrCbU9rtaYGvlkkRaW6ne_OmKAIM9rPfUxbhoncCZeinFRJAA2VE4kng9GsDd5LeKP6g2yHzfjczV_ZvbFWYqMapqRAo1Q0ofWqjF4x12W-05bkTQEw ). AI isn’t like that because AI has high cost of compute, if for nothing else other than for the energy. I’d written that question, and then suddenly it popped into my head, “Well wait, that’s really interesting as you think about data centers in space, because data centers in space eliminate the energy cost, and therefore become entirely fixed zero marginal cost businesses again”. Riff on that, if you would, and tell us about what you think first about is AI a winner-take-all business? So is there room for all of these different foundation models? And then how did the economics of this business and therefore the strategies change if data centers in space actually become a thing?*

Yeah, I think thinking about this, there was always a little bit of a cheat in talking about zero marginal cost businesses, because they weren’t actually zero marginal cost businesses. They still had to pay for data centers, they still had to pay for compute, but I think why it mattered was in the minds of management, it was a zero marginal cost business, no one actually thought about what’s the cost of one more customer. Of course we’re gonna have compute and that Google or Facebook or whoever already spent a ton of money on compute, it’s not like this is a new thing.

I think the difference today, actually, is not that AI takes relatively more electricity or that the chips cost relatively more, what it really is, is a finite amount of compute in the world ( https://stratechery.com/2026/mythos-muse-and-the-opportunity-cost-of-compute/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..tE8RH0NvWyPCP7id9reH1j__bfmyQQ9cqUhHBG-cAy_nQdS8Ty3ueA3w13-PSMQfZGtHOAat1BkbfDRGInb2Zd129KOzFk4hKcMGk4AVYfIeWLOLGJRN0UFYjqLzsXvL8Egoi8Sa-3jJ-85LpzR3sfqQpwOHabWpimFuk5f_0uK6L7VYbvWLYKk--T95wI82NfMxlWYv1zY1k_nQlMXh34En6Bs4zZce-yCjjt05KrK3toL1DTeqj_SWCjHh-ZDfZmoMD-kRNR2p926bOUDsxk8dX1jm0fUbTvBAf5tZ2uXlJJBhuzNDUQCHvVLLBcl06iG_5vMaNoC3hJ210hVLeQ ). In the zero marginal cost era, it was just assumed if you needed compute you could get it, and so I think the real change there is just overall supply. If you’re operating in a market where that doesn’t even enter your mental calculation — “Can we get compute or not?” — then you make a series of decisions, and I think this weighs on the enterprise versus consumer willingness to pay aspect that I think we’re going to talk about. So I think that that’s actually the more important consideration.

So is data centers in space ( https://stratechery.com/2026/spacex-and-anthropic-xais-two-companies-elon-musk-and-spacexais-future/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..fgARmzMtkwj79u_7Mr1oSvlTfA3ouOIm06-dwAUVLOZBmjG8Q-CGT53prjG3JtzZ0qDfXxTA74eAi9H_8A7vU-MHEXXOKEqfuoN5mmXpORxNGhEeXVJlWAtT-LmcEpAutGvJl51hlDxCnoL4dAyxQSe9fVWBxarmKtDrBu_M106CtRHms4MwGOav64KsN1BIOf_P_WuRxlfdkxYTZZvoMB94HxtJG4pSgB3z1yF7FdUNhzEFa_6nlC0d6qxGWOm441C1JPRySX2jDaEf0IffujTpr31fNYwHNRr7WLqxrGLnkx65hXof6Q_sHpVvSElI2bJR88KDg8bczoqhLJiWVQ ) a viable thing? This actually I think in many respects makes the case for data centers in space stronger, not weaker, because if the problem is not really the cost of energy, but just the sheer amount of compute in the world — if we’re already running into trouble sourcing enough power, but particularly sourcing enough spots to build, thanks to all the zoning regulations, the community pushback — there was gonna be a data center within a few miles of my house that just got killed ( https://dailyreporter.com/2026/01/29/deforest-cancels-qts-data-center/ ) , it was very close to being finished. To the extent [space] succeeds, it’s definitely gonna be a real condemnation of our ability to build. But the necessity of doing it is, it’s hard to talk about 30, 50 years from now, particularly if you’re thinking about stocks like next week, I get the luxury of thinking about two or three years, but we are thinking much further than that. And the question is, do we get to a world where managers are making decisions without considering, “Is there enough compute to do this?”, and we may need space to do that.

*CM: And you’ve made, staying on this topic of winner-take-all, is it a winner-take-all business? You’ve made the case that simply being capacity-constrained, if you’re [Anthropic CEO Dario] Amodei and you haven’t fully invested, is not going to be the basis of making this a multiplayer industry.*

Yeah, this was one of the easiest calls ever ( https://stratechery.com/2026/spacex-and-anthropic-xais-two-companies-elon-musk-and-spacexais-future/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..fgARmzMtkwj79u_7Mr1oSvlTfA3ouOIm06-dwAUVLOZBmjG8Q-CGT53prjG3JtzZ0qDfXxTA74eAi9H_8A7vU-MHEXXOKEqfuoN5mmXpORxNGhEeXVJlWAtT-LmcEpAutGvJl51hlDxCnoL4dAyxQSe9fVWBxarmKtDrBu_M106CtRHms4MwGOav64KsN1BIOf_P_WuRxlfdkxYTZZvoMB94HxtJG4pSgB3z1yF7FdUNhzEFa_6nlC0d6qxGWOm441C1JPRySX2jDaEf0IffujTpr31fNYwHNRr7WLqxrGLnkx65hXof6Q_sHpVvSElI2bJR88KDg8bczoqhLJiWVQ ) , I think. There was a whole, it felt a little astroturfed, two or three weeks ago, a whole chorus in Silicon Valley was like, “OpenAI’s going to win because they have compute, and Anthropic doesn’t, ha ha ha ha!”, and that was just a nonsensical statement. Do we believe in markets or do we not? At the end of the day, there is no entity in the world with a greater willingness or capacity to pay for compute than Anthropic. If they want compute, they’re going to get compute, and turns around and suddenly they’ve gotten a lot of compute.

I think, of course, there is someone that is now not getting compute, like xAI, but that is the market working. Now, there’s a lot more friction in that. Someone has to make a decision, “We’re going to not sell this compute over here”, or “We’re not going to use it for our own needs, the price is just too good, we’re going to take that price” or, “We have an IPO coming up”, or whatever it might be. So that weighs in favor of the Aggregation Theory broad idea that demand matters most, whoever controls demand will ultimately bring supply on their side. It’s not quite as clean, though, because deals need to be made, we’re talking about physical infrastructure. As soon as you get into the real world, there’s a lot more friction in the gears. But the overall direction, I think, wasn’t a surprise.

*CM: Is it a two-horse race now? Is it just Anthropic and OpenAI, enterprise and consumer? or is there still room for more winners?*

Setting aside the open source Chinese models, which you should not do, the big four in the US are clearly those two plus Google and Meta, and I don’t think either Google or Meta should be dismissed. The Google angle is actually really interesting with regards to the point we just made. Their Google Cloud numbers were unbelievable last quarter ( https://stratechery.com/2026/google-earnings-meta-earnings/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Rp0zAP1ZN2coZlfmbudvmffhA5V7Z2rg9oYneQl9hS3Aqtp87Ogt6lMAGpwLl1xXh3XFWoEOz6zBN4kkPZumdoGOjuGAkGleAKks_ba0vUYaFhtCgTEPN33zCEM8uZxkj2OfFi3ZGT5yrFCwBvaHbYwcfMt1Mq0Yr1UuYJZf-kUL9uxuOdsoif-DEGKHOpbgB1d8uz1EL-QJEPxufxjS3minF8HGX1bUTYzOh_kneWSlCeDJfiejlVAgjC8NhBeXrbT5OAfwH-GauQ4EnbkXkThZoi9W1EJWAa1BjtU9gFbAT_VwApgRt-ZG0j8XEJGZc1WiheSzGF4iKwUYGParbw ) , and I would really like to know what percentage of those numbers is Anthropic compute?

*CM: Right.*

But that speaks to the point. At the end of the day, and I asked [Google Cloud CEO] Thomas Kurian ( https://stratechery.com/2026/an-interview-with-google-cloud-ceo-thomas-kurian-about-the-agentic-moment/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..KYqGs1_UqNvG8mWRva6Ath6ZlxkzEzwsfSs6frCXPhqpekeG4fqRWW88rpn_ckbba31mP-Rh-IttXHRo1t7iSAKviQaFLtbl1wkzixX31vYiYF2Z_gwxPs6ey6abRoiizoGf0_FOl-nlX7-BHw_ja98X7eUwNtou1ooYiSQjAFb0DfgqQkWKaEgsFf-cIzqO64yzyq2wDd6zJetV-erxVnWm22dOAZfEiDvO006lBsQVjmqECMRtTDZRUfF71tL_jgTxDU24qRMc-MNIApFN-8s8aSH7wlQQNTWQlpzrWQP4wDHPiWqK9TaCX7OQo7iLzXHkFqlUo2zFtXy0_4Y9ew ) about this a couple weeks ago, when Anthropic does a deal to make their own TPUs, that allotment is already — TSMC’s already decided who’s getting that, so that allotment is coming from Google. It’s not running in Google’s data centers, it’s gonna run somewhere else, but at the end of the day, that’s Google making a market-based decision that, “We can make more money selling to Anthropic than saving all this compute for ourselves”.

What does that say about Google’s business? They’ll tell you it’s still doing fantastically. Again, the numbers certainly reflect that. But they’re very much in the game, their compute capability is important, it means they will probably succeed no matter what. The question is what’s going to be the margin on that success? Is that going to be selling infrastructure — that actually is where their compute pays off? Or is it going to be fully integrated up into their own models and applications?

*MN: I’ll rip the script up because one of the challenges we have on the analyst side is trying to think about the two largest customers of Anthropic/OpenAI supporting AWS’s build out and GCP. So put yourself in our shoes, right, or even Thomas’s shoes at Google Cloud. How does he know that in three years time, the money is good? He’s making a massive bet at Google to back Anthropic and OpenAI without long-term evidence that the money’s gonna be there.*

I think that’s a valid concern, but I think that case has diminished significantly in the last three months, I think there’s a decent chance that OpenAI and Anthropic do a combined $200 billion in revenue this year, and that’s real money. Does it match all the infrastructure spend that has happened? No, but I think the real challenge with all this infrastructure spend is, definitionally, a lot of it has to be spent several years ahead of time, and you see this in the opposite direction.

The number one reason we have a computer shortage right now is TSMC had no response to ChatGPT ( https://stratechery.com/2026/tsmc-risk/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..jWrN2IpQkqxec2IRgR2PrqyFNeL-_L5g1kG9NPJBvAK4cwy8hBsjuqLRIsGYsjljLE5BMRCtVLdPBesc4133ZdoeMscWJ0aS5A2KEU4r2hNBHcd7UytsQrBMYNa7M4hX_7d4ip_QHDMQNr12cj0LmCNCUCAAdwm8sBox70I1P4xTWcsN2d2N4-pslD4K7NjrI4oUiEpP9xeUk1iKciuDRHvP6si8PgmvdHT65X1GTYLg8VyAtI5vCcfasyBmU5W7A_FEtFd2xjZMEtmISYQWuxOuc4W8pwVNsXUWTQjMCYYKMG7eqmveBmJ41Isp4msswGIxzAa3R12qoPa0o8goNQ ) because their actual growth actually decreased the two years after ChatGPT. And even this last year, it was not up nearly as much as I thought it should have been. Now, it’s funny, it feels like they saw the light ( https://stratechery.com/2026/tsmc-earnings-new-n3-fabs-the-nvidia-ramp/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..FWhLF0H1RDHittf0SzCiQM2fI1JNY2vUqIoNQq7y0WJDS6Ecby1gjAtr-2-JsFL9zVH-xxA0RyMgk21FCne72BXJDgWBZmF6RyY0TBxDl7eQe2r4YFys72L_iN-4JI7Rrb1Oa2ABbQKTMTSCLF3EA110a4no1Sye1PwM2AFBB3iwVN2YiQ9EAbFzNjam6w1O_0-T5yT97NiZogUS6GrA_X5E3HGdxl-wweD3S6Ja2Wc_6xhqsXafThkGoeoi2fNP4hDmrUo27Si6OvU51sdNFCNDQggKuD3GJItb8vgBc6wQmu4K-4DqRP-tpaiO-jxW1Gtva94wAJEEdOaSBApzHg ) , [TSMC CEO] C.C. Wei was talking about agents on the last earnings call, which is probably helpful because they’ll start putting that pressure down.

But it’s funny for anyone who loves history of tech, this whole moment is so funny because you see all these ripples of past things that have happened in tech. You have the crypto thing, right? Everyone’s like, “Another thing in Silicon Valley that’s not going to change anything”, you have all the old memory cycles, you have other times when logic companies have felt hung out to dry because they over-built, there’s all these different parts of tech history that are impacting the current moment. You have also just the general push out of semi to Asia in general where, I lived in Asia for 20 years, it’s a different mindset in terms of risk-taking, in terms of thinking about jumping on opportunities. If all of silicon was in Silicon Valley, we’d have a lot of silicon build out for many years now, but it’s not.

But these are all short-term things, this is obviously the whole question of, “ Is it a bubble ( https://stratechery.com/2026/agents-over-bubbles/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..nIw0wLp9CtlMUgNSN6CVo4JTa2Ibk_0BGM5_xt_ayvf8P-ZHIEzb-8xrfTO5iKXJUIx9_woyFja53hKuPKdwP1zRCgVFqMrmoc1ZP42ru0NHEwRV1pf3LmsVfH4HA9GAZ-vi2cZlrJJbCorkL7U6AZuE0aSjj_KbPzMgXH9reo7bW7YomDhqRj5rTG80bOOBuDaavEgWOZMSt7x9rnzd1boVENxYN8-OSYYE-gnYrLvcN2z0KFIcLHsazC_1Ki2tpKp1mTKZrZsf1mjVewoZk_2NSBKkAyGYoW8zBQIedYajQFtA-13vBj-Mc14QmKU4fug-7oYWjhHD8lMXaJN85A ) ?”, or not. The long long run trajectory is clear, I’m sure there’s gonna be lots of lots of bumps along the way.

*Michael Morton: You’ve made a point that Google has the leading infrastructure for this movement for years and you’ve also, and I think in the last week, you wrote ( https://stratechery.com/2026/the-inference-shift/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..mU_4VGxfYjU_8AouQsq32QYRnCkHWbqZiFl987Nk_y4AalDaUCh4P85ap6tX47gfnf3achYYFwPYL3LdN2-HG3459mKPPJDvzbptyzaqrzRx4Klf378iUtPEQg3lSuEwH38_LXHkuAaJtthUZomc40IAP0VrzLKN_VBidss8ctCEKileUAaxQhqcjypfc2TtUFBbLCGLewmUk18nTjUqM3WPBVIblppbT1q0qLegUv6HG8sHYuyQRan596u0XMa0l1yxlP-T0n_nMmzmi4qerRunkPVoGihTwdY10VhzGlQczHAjAYqr-yiaOIkcFYybTP4SVFE7ffbFRXmV6mSXSw ) about how you’ve gone from answer inference to agentic inference. You and I were joking before this, about how we reserve the right to change our opinion on this subject on the walk home, because it’s evolving so quickly. Who do you think has the best infrastructure? The market pretty recently has unequivocally gotten behind like AWS infrastructure, some of those fears that have been written about in the past, not as worrisome. If you had to bucket some of the infrastructure players that you think are best positioned to be the winning providers.*

I’ve gotten pretty bullish ( https://stratechery.com/2026/amazons-durability/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..S1io2sPyeoAMEsbl_diJb1fu6jnjmNUEEtt5dfREvrm1nlCALcWsjA1JoUwSvve_-dcN5uJNVAOXeju9i6GPtUwmHHS6UT4GkyhKdYh4uUHuaxcyoAJbmqVzl8srJIo37bFk5zgKX_fJ69k_AeNzTQIz01RDsEALpr91lImTtbni3yer4XF2L0Ngpw9JdeNW0FBU_XSN1RXa3YfK6oG_nUcFe-Ffq6ERWfL-pDnaGP1F_2NuYVCmwvj-f-3a5S2qTWmEKEUOgV4hCNCWgBFXgR0aT18qJdTP0eyRoHJXZbBVMBT6lTVfNuIqKaPxPkvW2s0PgjHHKVkD_Zn8mzlMBg ) on AWS also, I think there’s a bit where they’re not competing with their customers is actually going to be pretty important. Like there’s no question — Microsoft had that issue back in January ( https://stratechery.com/2025/microsoft-earnings-coreai-mantleai-additional-notes/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..SgPM0EsJ_Bs7TbVjZ5sppabCwMf94cdprEZ42-5LCQDOI2IUmxs-2fdbKtxgjoepFTaqJOz97-LgubCAIwX_xfYExCojm-3OWgjJQ7gQ5ady2M-_kC4EEjPoIpJUmJ9F3qYcasC6oL7S3d-G-vb-fOcJcE-m5929s0XaU7wYiVcesVSMw26CLjI1djNpPtL3k05FK7fN5Ny4hzHSzIu5Cs6szvD5yZtQydP3fAyrPHNowaea51ocN84h8iz5shVBkAmomV5DvjV0J9GwNvTcn4NYNTznblcqnCevmSlb-AeG8KSpcNGpRcNr-lLK7d3tp8eBEsouoE9p40iCcPfIuQ ) where they’re like, “We missed our Azure numbers because we devoted more compute to internal workloads”, they’re telling that to analysts, if I’m an Azure customer, I’m like, “Wait, what’s going on here? Where do I rank in the stack?” — that’s just sort of a side note on AWS.

What I think is really hard to think about, what I was trying to write about this week, is not a short-term thing, this is in the long run of time, but if you think about all inference today is what I labeled answer inference. The most basic is a chatbot, you put in something, you’re waiting for a response and we had the experience, ChatGPT was quite good last year before the newest model launched — the base model sucked, but if you let it think it was good, but it took forever, it’s like, “Oh, we can use faster inference”. They got a new model, now it’s much faster and the quality is significantly improved. But there’s a real — when you’re dealing with humans, there’s a fundamental question of latency, how quickly do you get the answer? Just broadly speaking, if you put in a question, how fast do you get an answer? And even the coding stuff today is still fancy answer, which is you’re asking it to do a job, but there’s the human still in the loop, it’s going to do the job, it might have do a lot of self-verification, but you’re still the one doing the pull request, etc. As long as there is a human in the loop, then inference time really matters.

If we fast forward, though, to a truly agentic future where humans are not in the loop at all, agents are just doing jobs and something comes up, it might be event-driven, it might be a long-term sort of thing, and these things are just deciding when to run, deciding when not to run, that latency question’s gone. People talk about agents as in, “Oh now you have a million workers who never sleep” — well, part of the implication of never sleeping is you can deal with a slow computer, because there’s no cost to it. The human, every second, it’s costly that they’re waiting around.

If this happens, the way it looks — algorithmic breakthroughs can obviously change all this — but the way that is going to happen is going to be a huge memory issue. So everyone knows memory stocks, HBM — well, do you really need HBM? Actually you need like a whole hierarchy of memory, you might have, just like a CPU, you have memory hierarchy, you have L1 cache, L2 cache, L3 cache, the RAM, SSD, something in the cloud, spinning disk, whatever it might be. That whole approach is going to, I think, dominate this agentic inference and you’re going to lean more and more on high-capacity, but relatively slow memory hierarchies, which will diminish the importance of speed. The compute performance is still important, but it’s not the most important thing, that’s very different than certain training jobs or answer inference, and so the point of the Article is just to layout, once the human is out of the loop, your stack priority of what matters most totally changes. When is that gonna actually matter? Well, we need to get out of the total scarcity issue right now.

*MM: I’ve seen it even in my own behavior, setting some Cowork things up before I go to bed and just like letting it run. I’ll come back in the morning, project is done.*

Right, it makes no difference to you how fast it’s doing it.

*MM: I don’t care, yeah.*

But in the future, the Cowork agent is going to initiate those jobs on its own, and you’ll just show up and it’s like, “I have a summary of all the earnings reports that went down yesterday”, and at no point did you ever have any consideration or did the speed of response matter. It just mattered whether the job was done or not.

Consumer AI

*MM: If we’re on the subject of autonomous, something that you and I, I think, have agreed on ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-michael-morton-about-ai-e-commerce/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Pid9buRFM-qNwxba7KhzS6yF-yhIJ4_Pyxh3jkjJeh3eYMy6y3XlT031MJi-SxLyLPB1WOvZ7LPt1B5OvsCExFsObVIqFLXcIiu4EJgqMmvA0mYCS-JCMA691eoyWygVCulZDsG5gVomC9VTuDptOc9AjVt4nk8S4Xxy0fhcw2kb4Gjx8yfOSNG46RBrhhvrI6w1FnCRngp1FTiV3q4EVp4Bvd2dIv6a0Ng9BLc-YyqeJ-mXEf0-etkIGp7NQwRR10Zj6ujF40sXcayYN5TcC6b_hSzy_-YkWRsQH6GAA8bz7M2JrmweetccLYjLFf8KVntWIkvNjgUNvDpAkS0Yow ) is we don’t think that, at least I still don’t, I don’t know if your perspective has changed, I don’t think we get to like an autonomous agentic commerce world where all of your things in your house are just showing up and you didn’t you didn’t go buy them and you weren’t involved in the decision, I think you used the airplane ticket example. Michael defended Google while ChatGPT put up more users every day, multiples compressed, and now ChatGPT seems to run into a bit of a wall on the user growth when you have conversations with investors, the assumption is Google has won the consumer AI race, game set match. Now you have Meta, who is the question mark, they have three billion apps in people’s hands and they just put out a really good model. How do you think the consumer landscape adoption curve changes in what it looks like?*

I think the fundamental issue for AI in the consumer space, and this was the massive mistake that OpenAI made, is forgetting that consumers don’t care about productivity, no one actually wants to be more productive. The funny thing is, this is a lesson everyone in Silicon Valley has to re-learn every few years. I remember Dropbox had their, “We’re gonna be a consumer company” — no, you’re not. And they had to go through this extremely painful, complete re-work of their entire stack, where basically they had no new features for two years, because their entire structure was unsuited to enterprise-like access controls and all these sorts of things.

I think the fundamental divide is enterprises care and will pay for productivity, because that is a function of getting additional output from their assets, whether they be humans or computers, whatever it might be. Consumers care about entertainment, they don’t wanna be productive, they want to be entertained, and when you start from that premise, then I think a lot of stuff sort of backs out from that, and is why I’ve always been a Meta bull. Just in general, whether they want to or not — in many respects, I think Meta’s been challenged by Mark Zuckerberg’s personal preferences, he would love to be a productive company CEO, and he’s not. He’s an entertainment company CEO, they are an entertainment product at the end of the day, and that gives them a perspective and a mindset on this space paired with the best advertising business in the world that I don’t think they should be counted out ( https://stratechery.com/2026/google-earnings-meta-earnings/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Rp0zAP1ZN2coZlfmbudvmffhA5V7Z2rg9oYneQl9hS3Aqtp87Ogt6lMAGpwLl1xXh3XFWoEOz6zBN4kkPZumdoGOjuGAkGleAKks_ba0vUYaFhtCgTEPN33zCEM8uZxkj2OfFi3ZGT5yrFCwBvaHbYwcfMt1Mq0Yr1UuYJZf-kUL9uxuOdsoif-DEGKHOpbgB1d8uz1EL-QJEPxufxjS3minF8HGX1bUTYzOh_kneWSlCeDJfiejlVAgjC8NhBeXrbT5OAfwH-GauQ4EnbkXkThZoi9W1EJWAa1BjtU9gFbAT_VwApgRt-ZG0j8XEJGZc1WiheSzGF4iKwUYGParbw ).

*MN: Ben, I gotta ask you, keep going down that road, because you spent time with [OpenAI CEO] Sam Altman in the beginning, you called it an accidental consumer company ( https://stratechery.com/2023/the-accidental-consumer-tech-company-chatgpt-meta-and-product-market-fit-aggregation-and-apis/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..IDKt4-1kCep4zzKvsztRngXuIiUdynhOLPqHFoihmeeDjQoCNK0PhOOHxqZ8BSszYaNLTC7xN4qlDKJwpzFwUGq9peLz9X9tks43CcocGy9MpsLWe5qOFjdlOsYlvkZmMLxrSPUVefjZOhC0k6Jq6Wz6eolPphFFO6MNKUwrL4Qzfqx6cr1PFvqqt3UIl-wtX_Jjw2mhl8UY-0SOjK88BEINynTmUSBVdS6_Gt81lQ6CGZI9N-i965y0iy8EQU4GnUxgNuDoowfm13Lr8IM2x1uvS4RtY9UBAYvs8yyjzZED0IG7BHIi5GOpfD09ELswKgXQOADCB6V546EnQ97jCA ) , you’ve urged him to do advertising ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-openai-ceo-sam-altman-about-building-a-consumer-tech-company/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..sAz5ArMEv_RlhvIVvHsOa3AZZsdfXroWJbe2zkocx9ziC85QYtXuQkpJN9kpz3GTi1RL7zTWvtsGu2-dElV8QYLMCb0vP5Xbq254GSwUsEy22hl3GIWQ7AE7QrzPQZBUwImTzh-D7_B6vpOXmWDzvKPCdAQh7EoN9OjayfPoTHQLJPQeKQQn0BvcXkrdS7DjkBPwMeQwxFjs7TTPPgGXF3n4AEdk3QmTu3JUerXOnPzzUnPyV84BvOvFBxy1YZXe8rhizzhX9apdf1l5X9kt7ZEcIlJEYd0orUw_O-Z2B3K_bEl0sSFkviLhAFjwnksa1vYu8Yhy3l_WKkQmZE3psw#advertising ) , you’ve given him the playbook but it seems like he just can’t get his mind around it. I know they have people hiring to work on ads but what’s your take on how they’re positioned today and how they monetize in general between enterprise and consumer?*

I think in some respects they ran out of time. I think it’s a very different world if, in 2023, they slap up Punch the Monkey ads — that’s dating myself — but you just put something up there and you start to figure out what works. There’s an open question, are search style ads or are Facebook-style display ads, which one’s a better fit for AI? In some respects, it’s a search type, you’re focused, you’re task-oriented, being surfaced what you want and someone happened to pay for it, that might work well. There’s another thing where is that going to give consumers a bad sense of the reliability or trust in the results? And that actually, you should just show them stuff they happen to be interested in. Or is it a case that actually those ads only work for an Instagram experience where the ads are content? It doesn’t really matter if I’m seeing a Reel or seeing an ad, I’m not being productive, and so that’s when advertising, I think, works the best.

The problem is it’s 2026, and we don’t know the answer to this question, this is an answer that they could have figured out years ago. Once you figure out a direction, then you can actually start building out all the infrastructure around that to monetize. Having all the consumer attention in the world is only worthwhile if it actually leads to a payoff, and what the payoff should have been is they have a monetization engine that no one can match. If they had a meaningful ad business that was generating real revenue, that revenue goes into R&D, that gives them a sustainable advantage, which makes their product that much better, and you get this sort of virtuous cycle.

Right now, I think the reality is the consumer penetration of ChatGPT has been a massive negative for them. They’ve had to carry this massive user base, all these costs, they complain about not having enough compute. Well, how much compute is going to this huge customer base that doesn’t pay you anything? Has that slowed down your development? Are you on side quests ( https://stratechery.com/2026/openai-buys-tbpn-tech-and-the-token-tsunami/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..KL55gNwkpiZNl0SlhF4iYQbq51cEZYfAj68FFD3useed5-Uvsc7s9Ggfr5A-hQpoX4RjyWRnDEiLKASsQw2o1JKKTsdgjlVLZt052PLSTtSsbsI0br4EEcWwZpvuOJDHImIYIgn_o2UJ_L0Un5wAbm2DVfiRrpfVxXvRWFfMJjR_8de3_gbfamKx3BVOe5VInJl8jKaMxQNnzxYDl3wC_zDLFR7nbFWv0NukQkNG_BONwmCm63kVP3NV0TCiC2q687JkfqAqQn8TDciqgUcKZnBbi7lxqbjpag-SwBXtw7LLy18Jrw0xtRIX5qsExEFf44hNIXHRhkvdSeulQkUUcg ) ? All these sorts of things.

My read on — I thought they should have focused on consumer, it’s so rare to build a consumer tech company, last one is Facebook, right? Now, you could argue that’s wrong, “They should have focused on enterprise”, but the debate here is they should have focused. I think what my read is what Altman got hung up on is the cost to build one of these models is so large and is forecasted to only get larger, he felt they had to get maximum leverage on their R&D spend, and that meant because the models can go to everyone, they need to go to everyone, so we can monetize everywhere. And there’s a bit where, let’s really nail monetization in one place first I think would have been a good idea.

*MN: I’m actually looking at Mike, because the advertising monetization of this is one piece of it. But what it does to the consumer funnel, I think, is fascinating. Mike, why don’t you explore that a little bit? Because that’s been the topic of I think your best research over the last year.*

*MM: I have a follow up question for what Ben said, I think I can guess his opinion. We had MercadoLibre on stage earlier and they’re the first ones to, a lot of the companies have dodged this question and you and I talked about it on your podcast is, this seems to unquestionably lead to a compression in the funnel. We’re getting better answer machines, what does it mean for the ad ecosystem? What they say is higher conversion rate, it’s compressing the funnel, but the cost per CPC [cost-per-click] goes up because the conversion rate is so much higher and it’s gonna go up enough to in theory offset the lost clicks and these are sold on a click basis. The question for what ChatGPT might have gotten right or wrong, and every time I listen to them speak, they were talking at Shop Talk, they wanna make sure everybody is aware, “Hey these are separate tech stacks, your answer and your ad are not tied together”. I had dinner with Eric Seufert ( https://x.com/eric_seufert ) , and he’s like, “They’re too worried about this, they’re too online, the consumer doesn’t care”.*

“Too online” is the most accurate and consistently correct critique of OpenAI.

*MM: Do you think the consumer, because if you ask at your Thanksgiving table, how many of your aunts and uncles think your phone listens to you? A lot of them would say yes.*

And no one cares.

*MM: No one cares. So if OpenAI is giving you answers directly tied to your query with really great ads, that are so clearly tied to the query, do you think the consumer cares? And is that a misplaced fear that these AI companies are taking right now?*

I don’t know. I totally agree that the revealed preference of consumers is they don’t care about privacy, they don’t care about ads, they do care about paying, as in they don’t want to. It was just nuts to me that they’re like, “We’re gonna actually build a consumer business from subscriptions”, and to be fair, they make a lot of money. They probably make the most money — maybe YouTube Premium might be competitive — from a consumer subscription, you can put Netflix in there. They sold a lot of consumer subscriptions for AI, which is actually a testament to how genuinely useful this is, there’s a revealed preference right there. But at the end of the day, the vast majority of the people in the world, particularly if you have an effectively subsidized alternative, like say a Gemini, people aren’t going to want to pay, you need ads.

Will people care? My sense is history says probably not. Again, it would have been nice for them to have answered this question in 2023 or 2024 instead of 2026 where it’s a little late. With the funnel compression, I think Michael [Morton]’s been pretty right about this and just in general, if you think about general defensibility in a world of AI, all this stuff is magic when it’s dealing with anything digital, the moment it touches anything physical — this is why I’ve always said Aggregation Theory, even before AI came along, doesn’t apply to any physical businesses. Even though Amazon has all the characteristics, I’ve always excluded them just because you need a line. As soon as grit gets in the gears, these whole flywheels fall apart.

That’s another way to say these companies have moats, at the end of the day, will we get to a world where I won’t flip over to the Amazon app to buy something? Because it’s too much effort to go from ChatGPT to the Amazon app? Maybe when it’s all voice, you could imagine something along those lines. But at the end of the day, who’s gonna actually build the physical warehouse and delivery infrastructure to get those goods there? If in the value chain that is the scarce resource, then even if they do have an API or surface some sort of method elsewhere, their ability to extract value from that transaction is going to be very large. This is business, you make money where something’s scarce, and I think that Amazon’s delivery capabilities are a pretty scarce resource.

*MN: But in the attention economy, and this is, I think, one of the points that Mike has made in his research. In the attention economy today, the monetization of that infrastructure and delivery infrastructure, especially outside of Amazon, but at Amazon as well, is actually through advertising for a lot of these people.*

The only way to make money from attention directly is advertising because you have a perfect alignment of incentives. The advertisers want to reach people, you reach a lot of people. But that doesn’t mean it’s the only way to make money, there’s a lot of companies that have a lot of attention that lose a lot of money.

*CM: But the ones that depend on that attention today, I always think of your example of Chewy and how much of Chewy’s revenue really comes from advertising, not from selling dog food, it’s selling the ad next to it before you click. What the hell happens to that and what happens to those companies? How do they compete in a world where agents suddenly are the ones paying attention instead of humans?*

There’s an implicit assumption that the humans cease to exist. I mean, the humans are paying attention to something. So the trick and the question, and to go back to Meta, it’s a reason to be both bullish and bearish, is it’s an existential question. The question is not, “Will you figure out AI?”, the question is, “Will you retain customer attention?”, as long as they retain customer attention, however they do that, they are going to be fine. I think AI probably certainly plays a role in that, but unless we’re saying humans are going away, attention is not going away.

*MN: Are humans going to be spending their attention on what are essentially e-commerce sites?*

I think humans love to shop. You could look at a luxury mall or a luxury goods district, but we have examples of like, especially some of these apps that are super low price, part of the reason they exploded in COVID is people had nothing better to do. I think there’s just a — I’m a bull on humanity ( https://stratechery.com/2026/ai-and-the-human-condition/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..K9K-ChE2Ow5z239V9mu7-6xGVaixkgTOjnXc35UaOrIrU4cQzNPn0cBOayM02YGlbW1rh9JWl7r68Il5ZIp0GQHZewwwgUwbTZe6Qe-rorjiJxfxIoslGtWqNRLumfRqycWSQGK67ev48sIDwkzOFZKl73Kl1IObLMi1OkMyNuJC4eq1qB1LkyEf9cC7BOEbjWdcEEDhgrX_jcuV-4-rbG8iBAxMisjZuJH7zQ-Q3j_nps5ppvHWhnml4mxVwVQocY1W_ZqKAJ32D3dqP2MS_1s9GNYGYQR7ekPgnEbnzT9yuGxFaYi2EnLrSmzP11oRRfWZPhLQQsyzLYVlycoVQw ) in many respects in that a lot of what is viewed as our worst aspects and biggest vices is precisely what makes us interesting and a viable market in the long run. If all we cared about was being productive, OpenAI’s strategy would have worked and they’d be doing much better now.

*MN: So let me take your answer because I know you’re bullish on Meta, and you heard the last call, [Meta CEO] Mark [Zuckerberg] struck a more positive theme on humanity.*

Well, I don’t know, whenever I read through all these calls and I have an outline application where I take interesting quotes, I put a little header about what’s interesting about it so I can go back and reference it. I think I had three distinct sections from that call where my header was “trust me”, so I can understand people being nervous.

*MN: You’re bullish, and I’ve been bullish for years, the past year I’ve kind of cooled because I don’t I don’t get where the AI spend’s going away from ads. The ad proof is there, you and I write about it all the time. But the non-ad spend, the Meta AI vision, help me get more comfortable with, like, the “trust me” out of that.*

This gets to the reason to be both bullish and bearish. Meta is a purely digital business, if you’re a purely digital business, you have to be investing in AI, it’s an existential thing, even if you don’t know how it’s going to pay off. So for one reason, that’s a reason to be bearish, because they have to spend billions of dollars, even if you don’t know where it’s going to go. Because if they don’t spend it, they’re for sure in trouble in the long run.

The reason to be bullish is it’s existential to them ( https://stratechery.com/2026/meta-earnings-turning-dials-zuckerbergs-motivation/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..bnAzmMXVkVNZ9_lbGT8vBW2v2VRZ0wIxN6pEHY5MG8ZQsYEJQ_QAhku_G7omqMjrhwZV9kvT7Ir0wC233p5hjgTy5MYsCM4Ul4W50gXSThaO2dMe3xpiaCD5pTNtfbk4icPwpVJA1zME9Y7jtRNHUQ832CZNK6HMBok0iRjSy05WirIDJn_4TkfibCSi4TORmxSFi1G3mcNnl3c8MkV1VY1t5eI6Pfiwy35Ot-8hAN5qRa3dB0jIZ9SCF1oDBfsKPz0FGPLLlrynFkl0FFoz1y7hEfnIAs8svzPzzWFpN7OnT9olb2jKWqVYqPPPB8edPE1LARFdRjDxKA3fsfMVEA ) , they have to figure it out, and they’re the only company where the consumer market is their market. There is no, “We’re going to get saved by Anthropic buying up all our compute”, they don’t have a cloud business, which is a reason to critique them, but also in some respects a reason to be optimistic. First, they have to figure it out. Secondly, they’re going to be insanely focused on the consumer in a way that no one else is going to be.

But number three, I disagree, and frankly, the last time I talked to Mark was 15 months ago ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-meta-ceo-mark-zuckerberg-about-ai-and-the-evolution-of-social-media/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..utWHvBojIqWVZ0qkkfmYWlnTyqQI3STe50_pA4F3WZLxsierI67pK19Ekw7mT0BpbOoa0-TtAMwyS4h3mGK0JrIeCspReN76OtI7nb_NepejT14vRSOhlUoF1iNQ6KW-jtzuYCr4k8_ozBRZCo0lUI-3tVsysY5OD9ZkmN6_OIjaCcXEoPcfB6Ph6oHGu6jSwNM5j9bR5JuH-ROIMKxad7G-oqLYexPpCpJ2H7G6mbv7PhEaj5ePpfRC2pwZgVE0NmvZiLb8Yt3_rcQ3ZLHdlyXWfdWuqly6cuAhy3nWwOeY0_aTVa8KxwSCaFg1cFpRc0EbMcvaySNnonztKgaXqw ) , and I found it a baffling interview, in part because I felt like the interview ended up me trying to convey to him how many AI opportunities they had ( https://stratechery.com/2024/metas-ai-abundance/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..SAx07CUDYN5yZqxGHuGhKw3Kz4K4s-tCcOvMusd9Mc-BbN4teXrYXR27kBPM4NlqrCMNaxGCHoIbxIOfMQP4wBQEmpsPhGGv2yMvE_XLLGjG1tYSaDZ99TCP4i2UIYO-gL8yXHbqnKQVtrzI5HoAR10bxlpJxiSXu4mNAzpKPO2r_s_hWEkOL6XEVVF0E5hndM33UCaTt3_CigGGjahaYXPXcHTCtnSEI0i3fLR542uxW3v-OJIBe7Kc_1vPqJm1pEsiEIvCHojAnTiO9-43323nR0ZXTgajWtGB1VtkaL6hZf2PMCUhYrwSw6PaiiMT6l1rlWRDn4a8LTU1aHPz2g ). A big problem, I think a fundamental problem Meta has is Mark, this gets what I was saying before, he’s never been into advertising ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-eric-seufert-about-advertising-and-ai/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..bh_0IA57W2_UvsxL0zEpD24LU0zvDK8yq2HhTpdQGM6nxLWvmSOtr89bwSdhugb9cuY9Pz4Fby4yafbbjjPz_JVFCFIRn0rCtjtMdKsHUMzgXUAd_Gngdy4M8toJn4s3hIFqxDs-5Xt8jhPsTvHZI5rf1UloP-uSM3MxTnAWawKhiNZxyHziLk57p8UXydAshAs_0uSG5rfeFywZBAYQvzJe6aG2_xN3p-LIjdhU-QgekNuvCsV7LCbYvqOu0z98XRW3PmU2lhhbIZhimpXDHiIUSUsXaVh_Vz7WnF8t7BgcswKAgn1g_XrNH7v-U2aLH8G27Whx4vUlYJlevGi5yw ).

And the reality is for Meta, every single pixel on their app is monetizable. Like there’s someone who should post a picture of us on stage here and whoever made my shoes should be able to, there’s an opportunity for them to put in, if Facebook labels this item of clothing, someone could see this picture, it can be clickable and we can go buy it. That’s part of the whole value of Reels or Instagram, is you’re just flipping through it and the reason you don’t mind the ads is because it’s all an unproductive waste of time anyway. If you’re buying something or you’re just seeing a video, your mindset is the same, that’s why it’s such an unbelievable advertising vehicle.

I wrote about this way back, one of my first Articles ( https://stratechery.com/2013/mobile-makes-facebook-just-an-app-thats-great-news/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Z4aQvpPMTKFUVnJ21JovLMybdpehH_td0Ls8wh5sittJI7ojpalo-douiGcHSb6G9ktRRnQ08kNAx9WM-KrwUvl0oCt8xQ1-coTU5eqZB6tlPnhu7yyOcM10bUb7f_Mu6Ds6RPNuwx-N0Z5nEgr6qg6l_z2y9TjVLvdz-zjXJ7BBojx-yiv5vOE9orbMSBzA5laRTUaLbXKY3PYzsGdKIBFxFsQyMoN_CjbN-qPwJtQ8-NVI65AfZXmdIrrkgYxvdntHgubK-okAYOhuGMAeif9nDaT4M7a50X4_LEulFANB4r04nCQScEYz5sEBHtsIPbasZkGsRFscFTWWcEi0mQ ) in Stratechery, was like, Apple saved Facebook from itself, because forcing Facebook to be just an app meant they could not do all this platform stuff, all of which the idea of a platform is you’re stepping back and letting someone else take center stage. By being just an app, Facebook had to be center stage all the time, such that even by back in 2013, they were serving ads where if you scrolled your phone slow enough — we were in a world where you thought you’d put a little ad next to content — they were taking over your entire phone with an ad, and consumers loved it! They used it more and more, and so they can lean so much more into this.

The reason to be bullish on them is we could have zero progress in AI tomorrow and their runway to monetize what they already have is astronomical in my mind. They have to see it and want it and part of the problem is, I think their advertising product is, of all the advertising products, is one of the biggest social goods, they’ve created an entire ecosystem of businesses that would not exist without Facebook. That’s why they’re anti-fragile ( https://stratechery.com/2020/apple-and-facebook/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..sC-IcHGGWuK0oxjLWXS8QePwwDmSMC_hG7r-Kd4MR357dlCV0fj7AkExzteft64pTxOIdCNjUq_6C4xvXZHK-eeVSrabFPKir75ps6DC7chKasuSfaNwpvLB5DjRifrX3xxwkf53z9RigCI_AGF_2ktx6mR9xXaZw3rMQRfyamRCFrqBzLk94aMpGJKtXLyEt3jU8yER_O9TIxP9c5L8rRMQhsPdOxZK3jYhqxPIEdQe6s9x6IyKUfTrE0o5o87fpJEzI6z9ma11UcvJojuWpJlHJaSydA8ct5lwqYCblAV-N_yGngZR3MmoPh7_sQNjp7R-qb9EKM67rYE9xz6aIg ) , because if the traditional advertisers boycott them, all these companies that only exist because of Facebook just advertise more and Facebook gets stronger, they get stronger, it’s a beautiful thing. And the problem is Mark has never fought for that, someone else in the company has always handled ads, it’s always been something else, he wants to focus on product — but their product is ads! We’ll see if they figure it out.

Apple

*MN: Craig, can you ask an Apple question?*

*CM: I do want to talk about Apple. Last year ( https://stratechery.com/2025/an-interview-with-ben-thompson-at-the-moffettnathanson-media-internet-and-communications-conference-2/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..FfgWb6ut7yoe59x8SJ8AuCI0K__AMOswhxf8ir_p3_WUUEn8Khy3g5_yDFGuxag8hdmsVmoWZBO1Q1vxp16YoiwaEr87yA3XYGglnR2KRsi1AfmNUsTEO6GcEod4-HZ8LmRkONOjZekJXTRbx4ogeGfHXHH1SYCr9WiQszLMxXdgiVVOEaKcGK_c3HmFz4Tcgn9MS_7tq2RGFYLqb6TlIHW3RgTBwBHprMcJmWUBM-xnGBJsY48Te0W_PAWq5ZXhs-2T8B2limlN121AcmdNz8TypQutroUO_RAvgdAQp4FK034TW71QLMUXooL_8_vfVzZNIkeXNWagkL1a-3HQnw ) I think we talked a lot about Apple and what their AI strategy would be. I want to revisit that because Apple is today seeming to be almost AI model agnostic, notwithstanding the Gemini deal, they are kind of “Bring your own model, we’re good, we’ll try to make some money charging for our app payment processing or whatever it is that we’re going to make money out of”, is that a viable strategy? And what happens to the form factor? What happens to Apple if the form factor of the phone does get actually challenged by whether it’s glasses or something like that?*

Yeah, I feel like Apple has made their decision ( https://stratechery.com/2026/apples-50-years-of-integration/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..qjNBXzQbDWglbQ2xoJwGTBzwyDZTTlgjXNzdFhKdgG2PdkabB0_nZtqlkAM6-CpCmTYm0IbHl57YJXbrnTvwnsmrQkYsWFVIcubi4DU4JQaX26M-3DcjtG98X0VYhqWlffJPT1WZ_r59JgZ0chKDr4b2FrV6yHd9m9ex5W3UiaojS7Dy1p4aWisUC-HdbF3xBgczTfuNEpsgrbTGgAzZC3vxlJQKTTunwSuJ1Xzqr8DfDP_Vo8xeCT-v8WhZAszeBqUZ5sO6rjLSttg3Q6gCjqL65t9cspvU1CjUuwyBKTTeajGaM59uR0c4TbU2eV2C0jPvcOeRsBOFxk3z-MPAYw ) , whether they know they made the decision or not, when they decided to go with Gemini ( https://stratechery.com/2026/apple-and-gemini-foundation-vs-aggregation-universal-commerce-protocol/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..s1XKYFbzggSO3pcphjsIq1lNmgFBoywljD7AvcGXFxQrW7xiPWJnMZZUt6uY1MkwFaS5O0hM8t-zbsG2SFmHmkcvMk7iV6Qx-ZnJsWDT2-W7xNWbWReVqc-0pCBHArDgrJLYUgrhTKQL1nkuZwyEw6OeZJ8myDGoAvD6xWvZFNnxZR8HvwkDHq6psrWOEyT60J0wg0h8sdj3MslVVNwzAR25FnS9zOwe6yaHiblZXgPCIPfLZC6xj_FDFrJCEI61WSya1mtBDvIQtqBt8i_daJYAkTa7AwfUsorRFO6pGCHXBskSmi7CHPfGyt4Fh7mGmrlgAsldVg1iWuB9APuR6A ) as the basis for Siri. Obviously, they just appointed their hardware guy ( https://stratechery.com/2026/john-ternus-and-apples-hardware-defined-future-spacexai-and-cursor/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..FRzSWwOn5j0x8XBTLkd3o81hFuB--f0TtY5X0qNnbVQvTyL26iachlRFIze5IvZ2pMPvAxc6bcs66qRUb0OceEaMo7qLlj41BEwg9mIjhFIowlXhYKuq7Q85bbfRuth8aIXRWj8LLjRx4eSVob5acdlEYH2pvryYhPlye58_BJFEHgJa3ATlLRO-kH13xO4G59V93jPmjx1SNekD-PG0PSaYfijK13MDQsIGSqSDDtEOGbkquud23Xq07rDHeZuCjL78oxK_PmyLZcjH2Hd8LR2n22zxNKy2qwsx8SrfNRnCaC0w_3stNsUYEmR2V6nK0Ki_Q6m31dPvW3QWIpxCvQ ) to be the next CEO ( https://stratechery.com/2026/tim-cooks-impeccable-timing/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..fhXfY8bJWBrU-qtYy_iQIT1oCqkAXnbrQvfiXAFhboF4bpoH4I-f0bb4Coeg1YA1sEkKLcXJATe3Hu93aUnC8Jkm7mzWQMPacqy7kShsgj8CAmItRHyvBxnWtVm8Va4sf9bG1Vy7yG3vhshHPHmu7-A-D-RH-W0hpfaB_dvd69YtxjLd7qHlXp51UE2DUZf8jmey8FlMkNXvp8gsRaduBzBRzxl2BTSC2BvRL_j30-IhLz46I75DH4Q0kVfGjF0q4seC8TnSKrsCtxPI733hRQ7tPCkGQnTeC5TAxgX-UfPjiraIRCKqM-a8DQir5C2l6qepKKoNslNL6sN4dYSolw ) , and the decision appears to be, “We’re not going to be in the model building business”. Now, they’re still doing their own models, to your point, there’s small ones on-device. But as far as challenging an OpenAI — they’re not doing what Meta is, where whatever it takes, we’re going to be in the game, and I think it’s a reasonable decision.

Meta is a purely digital business, Apple is a physical business. At the end of the day, it matters that they do make devices. Does that mean they’re not in trouble in the very long run? For sure, the phone could go away. If the phone goes away, obviously that’s a problem. Then again, they’re good at making devices, they can come up with new ones. On the flip side, they’re so married to the phone, the app model, maybe the devices will be wrong, won’t figure it out, those are all fair questions to ask. But I think there’s a bit where I have a certain admiration for companies that recognize what they’re good at and what they’re bad at and there’s just no reason to expect Apple to be good at building AI, other than some sense that they should do that.

*MN: How much of Apple’s strategy is the realization that what they, at one time, and frankly what I believed at one time was a major core strength, which was customers trust Apple for privacy and that sort of thing more than anybody else, on device and all that sort of thing, versus the revelation that you shared earlier, which is Apple finally came to the realization no one gives a shit.*

What are they doing with Gemini? They’re layering on their brand, it’s not going to be labeled from Google, it’s gonna be labeled Apple Siri, so there’s a bit where they’re still monetizing that value proposition.

*MN: But the privacy thing is like, who cares, right?*

Some people do care. Apple and privacy stuff, I came up with a phrase years ago, everyone knows strategy tax, to Apple, it’s a strategy credit ( https://stratechery.com/2013/strategy-credit/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Wg5SPkK4h1q6iqJ1JBBwSt_iChuN5l4DDIQbc-ogLPhHgCC7mJQohBljfQN42NmBHUDkeCyctrU3MkW0zRqksYL0Iqd94wIzxEIGimEh_Zyz3qhz4LL4JJ-ADfMHdp7Ivr4sS8pEG5y3_jY6aGmTHGY4qU6RbRBPwJmS7v8H9oCtRoRIBjo5VGj1Zekg-c7ePubaQYLuM9ztmIb5KLVLoGmViDcLP1woePKNffQJ1j3tksFwqy1GWtAr2PekIC4fH0K6Tf3QRF3OuN4J0G79YnIgow4K7zaeOYoONHx8nO7mLfMcudZk1Vvorw8rJXGk0rKdWBT2-ypjXXvU3nQphQ ). They get to talk about how great we are about privacy and — this was way back in 2013 when I coined this — it’s like, “Yeah, you make money by selling devices” it’s like a freebie, right? You can make fun of Google and Meta all you want because you’re not in the advertising business and there is an argument that that ultimately constrains them because they don’t get all the data that they could to train models XYZ.

So did that constraint lead them down this road? You can spin this in any direction you want. You could spin it negatively. I kind of lean into the positive, which is that’s just something they suck at in general and I think it’s not an accident they suck at because what it takes to build quality devices is fundamentally different than what it takes to build quality Internet services, which are monetized by advertising. Like one, you have a ship date and it has to be good, it has to be perfect, you have to ship the best thing you can. And Apple never has recalls, they get it right, they get it out the door. And actually their operating systems used to be like that until suddenly you could update them to the Internet and the quality diminished because of it.

But with services, you can’t do that — this is why MobileMe flopped. They tried to ship it as a final product that was perfect and when you’re serving something on the web, there’s so many variables, basically infinite variables, what you have to do is learn how to fail gracefully. This is what Google is so brilliant at, and they brought that all the way down to the infrastructure stack. This, by the way, to circle back to the agentic versus answer bit, that is something to think about. We were in the dot com era, you were there, people were raising money to buy Sun servers. Google completely changed — how do we get to a world where compute was not even something you thought about? AWS brought it to everyone, but Google actually did the, “It’s all going to be commodity hardware, everything’s gonna fail gracefully, we’re just gonna assume stuff fails and work around it”, and that is a way of thinking about designing products and services that is the exact opposite of how we want Apple to think about designing their products and services. So there’s a bit about, I’m satisfied to the extent they don’t go in this space, because I want them to keep doing what they’re good at.

*MN: Mike, one question, then I’ll do the audience.*

*MM: My question is, following up on what you said about Meta, is Apple going to be the kingmaker? Does Siri actually take off to the potential it could be? For enterprise, I don’t want to use the word “obvious”, but it seems obvious to what we’re building to and what the use case is. But for the consumer aspect, when you talk to investors, “What is the Meta product?”, there’s reports that they’re trying to build their own personal agent ( https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/mark-zuckerberg-is-building-an-ai-agent-to-help-him-be-ceo-eddab2d5 ).*

I think the answer is the consumer product is entertainment. I actually think the enterprise space is much more uncertain, because to go back to what I was saying before about, actually if humans aren’t in the loop, what matters for compute fundamentally changes. If humans aren’t in the loop, what an enterprise is and how it’s constructed and everything totally changes. Like do you, and this is basic stuff, do you monetize by seat? Do you monetize by job done? Do you monetize by usage? We’re stuck on these very tactical considerations about this change that’s coming. When if you zoom out, the real risk is actually the entire architecture of corporations doesn’t make any sense in a world of agents. What corporation will actually exist in 50 years? I think it’s a much bigger question than, “Will humans want to be entertained?”.

*MM: I don’t disagree, from the consumer Internet analyst perspective.*

It’s good for you.

*MM: Where I get stuck is, and it’s constant debate, it’s where everyone is afraid to touch any of the consumer Internet app companies because they’re worried they’re going to get layered. You’re going to open your phone, and there’s going to be this front door to the Internet that you tell, “Book me a rideshare ride that has the fastest ETA right now or the cheapest one”, or “Buy me pizza from my favorite”, and I don’t subscribe to these.*

You’re dealing with a lot of people who are very enterprising and productive in their lives and don’t appreciate the extent to which their conscious thought processes do not map onto 98%.

*MM: Yeah, I tell them, you want to look at the Thai restaurant and see what Thai food you get.*

Yeah, and the other thing is all these companies, like your DoorDash or whatever, they have real assets in the real world.

*MM: Yes, they’re protected by the physical layer.*

And also, there’s going to be a huge number of stuff that’s going to benefit them. Things like drones, where are those going to plug in? This idea that if you’re coming in with a physical good that needs to be amortized, you’re going to want to go to the market that’s going to give you immediate usage right away. So I’m sure there’s going to be like someone’s going to be like, “Oh, Google Wing is going to destroy DoorDash” — probably Google Wing is going to work for DoorDash just like Google Cloud works for Anthropic.

Rapid Fire

*MN: So Ben, I have got 100 questions, you may have to do a Ben’s mailbag piece.*

Ok, I’ll keep my answers to 30 seconds.

*MN: Okay. This is not easy. Your view on enterprise software players now and the next two or three years? Anthropic has a press release, stock goes down every day.*

What was more fun, Anthropic having a press release and stocks go down or OpenAI last year having a press release and stocks went up? Probably the latter.

I would think about your wheelhouse, cable TV. There was an aspect where everyone could see where this was going, but it took arguably 15 to 20 years longer than if you just thought through the theory. Obviously, stuff’s going to be streaming XYZ, but the cable bundle persisted longer, not just because it was a bundle and all the benefits that were there, but like sports, advertising, big box retailers, our entire economy was actually structured around TV and sports ( https://stratechery.com/2016/the-sports-linchpin/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..nlVHR0VWFcB-L5WOGioqqa93ZvlpSr-yhbzSBNvyr_aDzWmOtRwm-ubApMpsP_tL2TqgP4sjhBYnPC8rRgizx-HW3fQwysG98zHOIBV9G8Kgpi2fzp45jEf7RClATTGuecM_VLHBgUFzI7sO56vute-LOlGUqCpcYVFat_jKZ2yEHKc9I8A5LCBe55ccTVsSr2ebxnQl7qfceEpqVuOlKhXx_A3kWF33Xyn4qxN4AS7D4XIT5EouumMtSBB3_fTrSFgE_lUel0hlwRSxaEkC9PiO5U-nIxUK0W9_kHBTidGA2V3zf4o0Uaqp_vZa44n5FqeUKzLvfyfgYUO8e0hXiA ). That meant that it was way stronger than it was, but it’s like the proverbial oak tree in a storm, at some point that things can come uprooted and then it’s going to be destroyed and I think a lot of enterprise is going to be way more persistent and way more sticky than you think. But also long-term danger where I think the concerns about terminal value are real, not just for the software but for the companies using it because those companies are gonna be undone by AI-native companies.

*CM: The bigger threat may be on the demand side, not on the supply side.*

I think they’re all gonna go up and down together.

*CM: All together, okay.*

*MN: What are the silicon implications of the agentic inference future? Who wins on the silicon side there?*

Part of what’s going to drive this is, just to use as an example, this is not any sort of long-term stock preference or whatever, but think about HBM memory. The reason we have HBM, HBM is just stacked DRAM and the reason you need it is you need huge amount of throughput to keep the GPU fed, because you have all this compute that you want to occupy. All of which is — again in training it’s super important because you have all these things tied together, you have to all stay together — but if you’re getting in inference jobs where time doesn’t matter, suddenly just using DRAM and then a fast enough processor, if we unbundle some HBM to actually be a bunch of DRAM that we can get produced more cheaply, this is also a very China-positive story I should note. All these things that go into — like they can make DRAM, they can make slower chips, they can make all these bits and pieces and obviously everyone wants faster. I’m not saying fast isn’t important, of course speed matters, but everything’s a trade off and in a world where the largest growth is going to be agentic workloads with humans not in the loop, I expect that to be commoditized, and I think China’s gonna have a big role in that commoditization in the long run.

*MN: Here’s a small question for you, re-industrialization in America. How real is the on-shoring of industry? If so, will it be primarily because of robotics and AI, will it work?*

Who’s making the actuators in the robots? China. There’s obviously a real chicken-and-egg question. I don’t know that this can ever really be accomplished outside of a war, which obviously I would not like to see happen. That’s not to poo-poo the efforts, I actually think things like fabrication happening in the US, even if the parts coming from China, that’s a center for, it’s like the grain of sand in an oyster that will form a pearl, that is something to build. One component supplier could build, like Amkor building a packaging plant in Arizona ( https://stratechery.com/2024/the-status-of-just-walk-out-tsmc-gets-chips-act-grant/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..Ly4XIPdlrywD-WVvC9HPatdiOc4V6q1YDei7nCuziQxZntF8XLNj_k4lZmEOk4C7_p31LWepv1ZF42g5Xo1r20KPF_AqMs_zdpnciz6QMVqVSYzdxf3mVBA3WsXbL0X9ChOhKlPu8qxgiKRzAwE0BuxA7KN10af_KQtYdlA8aNYmFe0JBMw-mx5waFSBXmuA6abzo5kacKlXL1xzlxDgA-ToSG4-ufSkoCD4TF7fNz4vvL8bxvIDb1WdDE-eeXoeLMZq8DfPSgOGNLGgCQMBt1P-0bTiUFrN2-sBpOheTRRQpsGkGTsEdFxiX6ttYol8AO_kmW_DUugAjbacDpYgdw ) next to the fab. You can imagine that being the seed of a cluster. It took us 50 years to move everything to China, it’s going take a long time to move everything back.

*MN: What’s Meta’s real end game for data center builds? Can they be successful at Gen AI? Do they have their own xAI moment at Meta licensing data?*

It’s possible, but that’s a reason I think to be somewhat — it’s a reason to be short-term optimistic about the stock, they could turn around tomorrow and sell all the compute that they’re building. I do think that’s a reason to consider the terminal value though.

*MN: A question that I wouldn’t ask you but I’m going do anyway. What’s your take on — do you worry about Google Search revenue and why?*

I’m always wrong about Google. Their ability to wring revenue out of Search appears to be infinite so I’m going to give them the benefit of the doubt until it ever stops being the case. And the reality is — speaking of latency, Google is a service where humans are in the loop — you want the fastest answers go to Google, even the AI answers. I use Google Search for AI much more than I did previously, in part because it’s a basic thing that’s giving me an answer right away. That is them absolutely capitalizing on what they’re good at and a reason for optimism.

*MN: This is a good segue, it’s about you. How do you incorporate AI into your workflow day-to-day? What has been the biggest change in your day-to-day today versus pre-AI?*

This is an interesting question. People wanted me to spin some sort of productivity story and I feel like I’ve spent so much time optimizing for my personal productivity a long time ago, all of which just stripped everything to the basics. I work all in text, basic text files. I feel like a lot of people have traded one set of productivity porn, which they’ve gotten stuck in, and now AI solves all their productivity problems again. So day-to-day in that regard, I use it all the time for basic stuff, of course, asking questions, things like that.

As far as writing goes, where I think I’ve always had the good fortune which I could write a lot is I only do one draft and I copy-edit it, but it’s fully thought through. When I’m stuck in a section, I do all the research, think it through, write it out, and it’s a laborious process, but when I’m done, I’m done. However, there’s some Articles more recently where I will just sketch out, I have the argument in my mind, so I write out more of a rough draft before and then feed it to, and I always feed it to both, both ChatGPT and Claude, because you do get sometimes very different things, and almost always, or particularly ones where I think of a particularly innovative idea, they’ll attack it, which I think is a positive, because it’s a non-consensus sort of thing.

*MN: It’s never been said before.*

But it just dramatically speeds up the details, making sure I get all the specifics right about some technical implementation or whatever. I’ve actually, in some respects, I’ve actually moved more to a rough draft/final draft approach for some Articles. Some I just know exactly what I want to say and skip the AI.

*CM: Here’s the way I want to ask the final question, because I think the place we always want to wrap up is the bubble question, but I’m gonna ask it in a specific way because you already said the cumulative revenues of Anthropic and that sort of thing suggest maybe it’s not a bubble.*

Right, but saying it’s not a bubble is the number one indicator of being in a bubble.

*CM: But what if compute isn’t scarce? So suddenly the marginal cost of competition drives down the price of AI, even for enterprises. What happens if compute is no longer scarce? Does that make this bubble pop?*

I think the compute not being scarce, given that all the energy is in semiconductors, in particular, we’ve seen this game before. Eventually we had too much capacity, they have all these fixed costs, and then it’s going be the semiconductor down cycle of all down cycles.

Ultimately though — this is kind of a cliché answer — once we have abundant compute, then anything is possible, right? Who would have thought to create the very idea of, just to go back to Meta, a Reel, is so absurd and is so downstream from excess bandwidth, excess compute, all like excesses in basically everything that goes into the fact that people are making stupid videos and posting them online and they’re getting sorted and consumed, the amount of excess that goes into this product is ridiculous. The potential of what can be done once we have infinite compute, that is when the true AI era will be here.

Is it going to be different this time? I think in this respect, probably not. At some point, there will be the crash, whatever it is, but that doesn’t mean what’s here isn’t real. But who knows, maybe it’ll be different this time. We’ll update you next year.

*MN: Always such a pleasure to talk to you. I know all of us in this room would say we wish we could go on for another hour, but thank you for being here.*
